name: ALAS — Adaptive LLM Agent System (2025, Stanford)
origin_domain: AI research (multi-agent LLM planning, disruption-aware systems, transaction processing, workflow orchestration)
year_developed: 2025 (arXiv:2505.12501, May 2025 — 'A Stateful Multi-LLM Agent Framework for Disruption-Aware Planning'; arXiv:2511.03094, November 2025 — 'Transactional and Dynamic Multi-Agent LLM Planning')
progenitor: Edward Y. Chang and Longling Geng (Stanford University, Department of Computer Science)
driving_insight: Standalone LLMs suffer from four fundamental planning pathologies: they cannot reliably self-verify (absence of self-verification), they lose track of commitments over long contexts (context erosion), they are biased toward high-probability continuations even when infeasible (next-token myopia), and they lack persistent state across interactions. Real-world planning is further complicated by RUNTIME DISRUPTIONS — a machine breaks down, a traffic delay occurs, a last-minute task arrives. The key insight is that global replanning (throwing away the entire plan and starting over) is catastrophically expensive in dynamic environments — the correct approach is LOCAL COMPENSATION: edit only the minimal affected region of the plan while preserving everything else. ALAS achieves this through a three-layer architecture where each agent is paired with a fault-tolerant compensator that defines recovery protocols. History-aware local compensation (Local Cascading Repair Protocol) beats global replanning on every metric: 83.7% success rate, 60% token reduction, 1.82x speedup.
decision_style: hierarchical with localized autonomy — the Workflow Blueprinting layer defines the overall plan structure; the Agent Factory instantiates agents with specific roles and compensation handlers; the Runtime Monitor coordinates execution in dependency order but agents make LOCAL compensation decisions without central coordination. The decision style is hierarchical at the planning level (blueprint defines the global workflow) and autonomous at the execution level (each agent independently handles disruptions within its scope). This hybrid avoids both the rigidity of fully centralized control and the chaos of fully decentralized negotiation.
problem_class_targeted: dynamic planning problems with runtime disruptions, where plans must be generated, executed, monitored, and repaired in real time. Specifically targeted at: (1) industrial job-shop scheduling — machines fail, jobs arrive late, priorities shift; (2) urban ride sharing — traffic delays, last-minute passenger requests, vehicle breakdowns; (3) event planning — complex constraint satisfaction with changing requirements; (4) any domain where LLMs must plan AND adapt. The problems are ill-defined in the sense that disruptions are unpredictable, but well-defined in the sense that correctness criteria (schedules, makespan, constraint satisfaction) are measurable.
ordered_phases: Three-layer architecture executed sequentially with runtime loops: LAYER 1 — Workflow Blueprinting: (1) Receives planning input (task description, constraints, resources, deadline); (2) Translates input into a structured workflow template with abstract roles, dependency edges, and temporal constraints; (3) Produces a canonical workflow IR (Intermediate Representation) that is engine-agnostic. LAYER 2 — Agent Factory: (4) Instantiates role-specialized agents from the workflow template; (5) Assigns each agent a capability profile, logging schema, and compensation handler (retry, catch, timeout, backoff, idempotency keys, loop guards); (6) Agents are paired with fault-tolerant compensators that define recovery protocols. LAYER 3 — Runtime Monitor and Repair: (7) Activates agents in dependency order; (8) Validator continuously checks agent outputs against constraints; (9) When disruption detected: Local Cascading Repair Protocol activates — edits only the minimum affected region, preserves work-in-progress, contains cascade effects; (10) Versioned execution log records all states for grounded checks and restore points; (11) Execution completes when all agent tasks are finished and validators pass.
feedback_loops: Multi-level feedback loops: (1) Validator-to-Agent: the runtime validator checks each agent's output against constraints — if invalid, triggers the agent's compensation handler (retry, catch, timeout, backoff); (2) Repair-to-Execution: the Local Cascading Repair Protocol modifies the affected region of the workflow and feeds the repair back into the execution stream without global rollback; (3) Versioned-Log-to-Validator: the execution log provides grounded check data for the validator, avoiding self-check loops (agents don't verify their own outputs); (4) Monitor-to-Blueprint: if disruption patterns recur, the blueprint can be updated for future runs — this is an explicit feedback loop from execution experience to planning. The key innovation is that feedback loops are LOCALIZED — a disruption in Sub-task A triggers compensation only in the affected dependency region, not across the entire workflow.
gates: Formal validation gates throughout execution: (1) Blueprint validation gate — the workflow template is checked for structural completeness (all roles assigned, dependencies acyclic, temporal constraints consistent) before agent instantiation; (2) Agent instantiation gate — each agent's capability profile is verified against the assigned role requirements; (3) Per-action validator gate — each agent's action output is validated against constraints before downstream agents consume it; (4) Dependency gate — agents cannot execute until their upstream dependencies have been satisfied AND their outputs validated; (5) Completion gate — all agents finished, all validators passed, makespan computed. When a gate blocks: the compensation handler activates at the local level; if compensation fails after policy-defined retry limits, the disruption escalates to the blueprint level.
exit_criteria: Success exit: all agent tasks completed with all validator checks passed, the final makespan/completion metric computed. Partial success: in disruption-heavy scenarios, some tasks may complete while others are still undergoing compensation — the system reports partial completion with identified disruption impacts. Failure exit: a disruption cannot be resolved by local compensation (all retry/backoff/catch policies exhausted) AND the disruption cannot be contained within the workflow (escalation to human or blueprint replanning required). The versioned log preserves all completed work — no work is lost even in failure cases.
decomposition_method: hierarchical, role-driven decomposition via the Workflow Blueprinting layer. The blueprinting step translates a natural-language planning problem into a structured DAG of agent roles with dependency edges. This is NOT LLM-driven decomposition (like RDD) but rather template-driven: the blueprint maps problem semantics (machines, jobs, vehicles, passengers, constraints) onto a workflow pattern (batch processing, sequential pipeline, fan-out/fan-in). The decomposition is domain-aware: a job-shop scheduling problem decomposes into machine-assignment, sequencing, and quality-check roles. The decomposition produces abstract roles rather than concrete task instances — the Agent Factory instantiates specific agents for each role based on the task parameters.
granularity_guidance: Decomposition granularity is determined by two principles: (1) Role isolation — each agent role should handle exactly one type of decision (assignment, sequencing, verification) to enable targeted compensation; (2) Compensation scope — the granularity must allow local repair to contain cascades (if two tasks share an agent, a disruption to one may cascade to the other unnecessarily). The leaf node is a single agent with a single well-defined role and an independent compensation handler. Empirical evidence: the DMU and TA benchmarks use 5-50 jobs with 5-20 machines, decomposing into roughly O(jobs) agent instances. Decomposition stops when further splitting would increase coordination overhead beyond the benefit of finer-grained compensation.
boundary_rules: Explicit boundary rules enforced by the workflow DAG: (1) Dependency edges define input/output contracts between agents — Agent B receives Agent A's output only after A's output passes validation; (2) Compensation boundaries — each agent's compensation handler operates within its scope; effects beyond the scope are handled by upstream coordination; (3) State isolation — agents do not share mutable state; state is passed via versioned, immutable log entries; (4) Idempotency keys prevent duplicate execution at boundaries. The workflow IR enforces clean boundaries: no overlap (each task belongs to exactly one agent), no gaps (the dependency DAG ensures every task has a path from input to output), clear ownership and handoff (versioned logs track provenance at every boundary).
risk_assessment_mechanism: Runtime validator-based risk detection: the independent validator (non-circular, fresh bounded context) checks agent outputs against constraints. Risks are classified by the compensation handler's policy matrix: (1) Transient failure — remedied by retry; (2) Persistent failure — handled by catch/fallback; (3) Cascade risk — contained by the localized repair radius; (4) Structural failure — violations of global constraints (makespan threshold, deadline) trigger escalation. Risk is assessed per-disruption by the validator and graded by: severity (local vs. cascading vs. global), recoverability (compensable vs. non-compensable), and impact (makespan degradation). The paper reports a validator overhead that is 'low' and that injected structural faults are detected reliably.
unknown_handling: When an agent encounters a situation not covered by its compensation policy (unknown failure mode), the default behavior is ESCALATION: the disruption is passed up to the Runtime Monitor, which may: (1) invoke a more general catch-all policy; (2) request human intervention; (3) trigger blueprint-level replanning (fallback to global replan). This is a graded response — local first, then escalation — rather than a binary halt/proceed. The compensation handlers are designed to be exhaustive for known failure modes, but the catch-all policy handles unknowns gracefully.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE at the agent level (actions that fail validation are blocked) with escalating permissiveness (escalation to human or blueprint replanning for unknowns). The validator is RESTRICTIVE — it blocks invalid outputs rather than allowing them through. The compensation handler's catch-all policy is also RESTRICTIVE (fails safe) rather than permissive. This conservative stance is appropriate for the industrial scheduling domains ALAS targets.
safety_guardrails: Multi-layered safety: (1) Validator isolation — the validator runs in a fresh LLM context with no knowledge of the agent's reasoning, preventing self-verification failures; (2) Compensation policies — retry limits, timeout, backoff, idempotency keys, and loop guards prevent infinite retry loops; (3) Versioned execution log — provides an immutable audit trail and restore points, preventing state corruption; (4) Bounded repair radius — compensation is localized, preventing one disruption from cascading globally; (5) Human escalation path — structural failures reach a human operator; (6) Engine parity checks — workflow IR compiled to both Amazon States Language and Argo Workflows with round-trip validation. Notable: ALAS does not specifically address prompt injection, tool misuse (tools are abstract in the scheduling domain), or adversarial inputs.
human_decision_points: Humans intervene at: (1) Blueprint design — defining the workflow template and agent roles (one-time setup); (2) Escalation handling — structural failures that cannot be resolved by compensation reach a human; (3) Policy override — humans can adjust compensation policies based on domain knowledge; (4) Final review — the completed schedule/plan is reviewed before deployment. The human is ON the loop (available for escalation) rather than IN the loop (per-action approval). The audit trail enables retrospective human review of all agent decisions.
override_capability: Humans can override at multiple granularities: (1) Full halt — stop the entire workflow; (2) Blueprint-level — modify the workflow structure and restart; (3) Policy-level — change compensation handler parameters (retry count, timeout duration); (4) Per-agent — replace an agent's output with a human-provided alternative. The versioned log enables human override at any point in the execution history, with the ability to replay from the override point. This is a sophisticated override model compared to most agent frameworks.
fallback_when_no_human: When a human is unavailable for escalation, the system applies the catch-all compensation policy: safe default (reject the action, mark the workflow region as blocked, preserve all other progress). The system does not auto-approve or proceed past validations — it halts the affected region and continues processing independent regions. If the blocked region is on the critical path, the overall workflow stalls. This is a 'block and preserve' strategy rather than 'auto-approve' or 'block indefinitely.' The blocked state is logged and can be resolved when a human becomes available.
max_problem_size: Tested on DMU and TA job-shop scheduling benchmarks: DMU01-DMU50 (up to 50 jobs, 20 machines) and TA01-TA50 (up to 50 jobs, 20 machines). Urban ride sharing (URS): multiple vehicles with dynamic passenger requests and traffic delays. Event planning: family reunion with complex multi-stakeholder constraints. Theoretical limit: the workflow DAG scales linearly with the number of independent tasks. What breaks at scale: (1) DAG complexity — as the number of dependency edges grows, the blueprinting step must handle larger constraint graphs; (2) validator load — each agent output must be independently validated, and validation cost scales with agent count; (3) compensation cascades — with very dense dependency graphs, the 'local' repair radius may expand to include most of the workflow, defeating the purpose of local compensation.
parallel_execution: First-class parallelism: agents at the same DAG depth with no cross-dependencies execute in parallel. The dependency DAG explicitly identifies parallelizable agent groups. The Runtime Monitor dispatches agents as their dependencies are satisfied (like a task scheduler). The Local Cascading Repair Protocol handles the interaction between parallel execution and disruption: a disruption in one parallel branch does not affect other independent branches — they continue executing. This is a key advantage over sequential architectures. Parallelism is managed through the workflow IR, which compiles to Argo Workflows (Kubernetes-native parallel job execution).
team_scaling_model: Linear scaling with task count: the number of agents scales with the number of independent role instances required by the problem. For job-shop scheduling with N jobs and M machines: the blueprint creates roughly O(N) agents (one per job) or O(M) agents (one per machine), depending on the decomposition strategy. The compensation handler overhead is per-agent but amortized (handlers are templates instantiated per role, not per agent). Team scaling is linear with problem size, and the localized repair prevents super-linear communication overhead (each agent communicates only with its DAG neighbors, not all-to-all).
cost_scaling_model: Token cost scales as O(N + E * c), where N is the number of agent executions, E is the number of disruptions, and c is the average compensation cost. The 60% token reduction over baselines comes from avoiding global replanning: a global replan for a K-agent workflow costs O(K * agent_cost), while local compensation costs O(affected_region * agent_cost) where affected_region << K for typical disruptions. Cost per successful plan: ALAS achieves higher success rates (83.7%) for similar or lower token usage compared to baselines, resulting in lower cost-per-success. The validator adds a constant overhead per agent output. The branching factor for disruptions is contained by the compensation radius: each disruption triggers O(radius) compensations, not O(K).
replanning_built_in: Yes — replanning is a first-class phase implemented through the Local Cascading Repair Protocol (LCPR). This is LOCAL replanning, not global: only the minimal affected region of the workflow is modified. LCPR is triggered by the runtime validator when it detects a disruption (invalid output, constraint violation, agent failure). The protocol: (1) identifies the affected DAG region; (2) applies compensation policies (retry, catch, timeout, backoff); (3) if compensation fails after policy limits, expands the repair radius one step; (4) only if the repair radius reaches the full workflow does global replanning occur. This is history-aware (uses the versioned log to understand what changed) rather than blind (throw everything away). The November 2025 paper adds a formalized localized repair protocol with validator-based runtime monitoring.
trigger_mechanism: Disruption detection by the independent validator triggers replanning: (1) Agent output fails validation (invalid schedule, constraint violation, unachievable assignment); (2) Dependency failure — an upstream agent's output changes (due to compensation), invalidating downstream agent's assumptions; (3) Resource change — a machine becomes unavailable, a vehicle breaks down, a new job arrives (environmental change); (4) Timeout — agent execution exceeds allocated time; (5) Structural violation — the emerging plan violates a global constraint (makespan threshold, deadline). The validator is the primary trigger — it continuously checks outputs against constraints and fires compensation when violations are detected.
adaptation_cost: Local compensation cost: affected_region * agent_cost + validator_overhead. This is typically MUCH smaller than global replanning cost (K * agent_cost). The paper demonstrates that local compensation preserves makespan better than global recompute under runtime perturbations. The adaptation cost is proportional to the disruption's blast radius — the size of the affected DAG region. For a typical disruption affecting 1-3 agents in a 50-agent workflow, local compensation costs 2-6% of global replanning. The versioned log eliminates lost work — agents whose outputs were validated and are not in the affected region do not need to re-execute.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Tiered recovery: (1) Retry — transient failures (network, API errors) are retried with exponential backoff; (2) Catch/fallback — persistent failures invoke a predefined fallback agent or alternative strategy; (3) Local compensation (LCPR) — if agent output is invalid but fixable, local repair edits the minimal region; (4) Radius expansion — if local repair fails, the affected region is expanded one DAG hop and repair is retried; (5) Blueprint replan — if repair radius reaches critical size or structural failure occurs, the blueprint is recomputed; (6) Human escalation — if all automated recovery fails, a human operator is notified with the versioned log. Blast radius containment is achieved by the localized nature of steps 1-3, and the versioned log enables recovery from any point without loss of validated progress.
output_artifacts: The workflow blueprint (DAG of agent roles with dependency edges), the instantiated agent roster (capability profiles, compensation handlers), the versioned execution log (all agent outputs, validator results, disruption events, compensation actions), the final plan/schedule, and the makespan/completion metrics. The November 2025 paper adds a conformance matrix and engine parity tests. The audit trail is comprehensive — every agent decision, validation, and compensation is logged immutably.
briefing_format: The workflow DAG is the natural briefing artifact — it shows the agent roles, their dependencies, and the flow of work. For human operators: the DAG visualizes what happened, while the disruption log highlights what went wrong and how it was repaired. The versioned log compresses to a high-level summary: tasks completed, disruptions encountered, compensations applied, final makespan vs. target. The paper's evaluation includes makespan comparison tables and disruption recovery timelines. For operational use, the briefing format would be a dashboard with the DAG, disruption markers, and compensation traces.
traceability: Excellent traceability via the versioned execution log: every outcome traces back through the agent that produced it to the validator that checked it to the blueprint that defined the role. The audit trail preserves: the agent's input context, its output, the validator's assessment, and any compensation actions. The rationale for agent decisions is implicit (the LLM's output) rather than explicit (a structured decision rationale), but the full context is preserved for retrospective analysis. The versioned log enables 'time travel' debugging — replay from any checkpoint to understand why a particular decision was made.
agent_communication_protocol: Engine-agnostic workflow IR that compiles to Amazon States Language (ASL) and Argo Workflows (Kubernetes). Agent communication is through structured state passing (versioned log entries) rather than direct message exchange. The workflow DAG defines the communication topology: agents send outputs to downstream dependents via the shared log. No direct peer-to-peer messaging. Discovery is handled by the Runtime Monitor (agents are assigned to roles at instantiation). Communication is synchronous (agent outputs block downstream agents) but asynchronous across independent branches. For A2A/ACP/ANP integration: the workflow IR could be mapped to any protocol that supports task delegation and result aggregation. The November 2025 paper emphasizes engine parity — the workflow IR is independent of any specific protocol or runtime.
learning_memory_architecture: The versioned execution log serves as episodic memory — recording what happened for each workflow execution. The feedback loop from Runtime Monitor to Workflow Blueprinting enables procedural learning: recurring disruption patterns can inform blueprint improvements for future runs. However, this is a manual/design-time feedback loop, not an automated learning system. The paper does not describe automatic policy improvement, fine-tuning, or pattern extraction from execution logs. The compensation handler policies are static (predefined by the designer), not learned from experience. This is a significant gap for long-term improvement.
latency_bounds: Near-real-time — the paper reports millisecond-level response times for the Local Cascading Repair Protocol. The system targets operational domains (ride sharing, job-shop scheduling) where disruptions require rapid response. Total execution time depends on the number of agents and the critical path length. Agent execution is LLM-call-bound (seconds per call), but the compensation decisions are policy-driven (milliseconds). Under time pressure: reduce compensation handler retry counts, tighten timeouts, or accept lower-confidence validator outputs. The paper does not provide detailed latency distributions.
evaluation_benchmarking: Evaluated on: (1) DMU job-shop scheduling benchmarks (DMU01-DMU50, up to 50x20 jobs/machines); (2) TA job-shop scheduling benchmarks (TA01-TA50); (3) Urban Ride Sharing (URS) with dynamic traffic and passenger disruptions; (4) Event planning (family reunion) with complex multi-constraint scenario. Baselines: standalone LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.7, DeepSeek R1, Gemini 2.5 Pro), traditional optimization solvers (OR-Tools, Google CP-SAT), and transactional LLM planning frameworks (SagaLLM). Metrics: success rate (83.7%), token usage (60% reduction), execution speed (1.82x faster), makespan degradation under disruptions (less than global recompute). Additional metrics: validator overhead, repair radius under perturbation, compensation policy effectiveness.
mapping_strength: Very strong mapping to JORDAN pipeline. The three-layer architecture maps directly: Workflow Blueprinting -> plan node (task decomposition into agent roles); Agent Factory -> execute node (instantiation with tool/skill assignments); Runtime Monitor and Repair -> replan + risk_assessment nodes (disruption detection, localized compensation, gate validation). The versioned execution log maps to JORDAN's traceability requirement. The validator-based gating maps to JORDAN's approval_gate node. The compensation handler policy matrix provides a template for JORDAN's error recovery strategies. The engine-agnostic workflow IR aligns with JORDAN's need to support multiple execution environments.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) ALAS targets scheduling/logistics domains with well-defined constraints — JORDAN targets open-ended software development and research tasks where constraints are fuzzy and correctness is harder to validate; (2) the compensation handlers are manually designed per domain — JORDAN would need automated generation of compensation policies for diverse task types; (3) no tool use beyond the scheduling domain — JORDAN agents need web search, code execution, file I/O; (4) the validator relies on checkable constraints (makespan, deadlines, machine availability) — JORDAN tasks lack clear constraint formulations (what does it mean for code to be 'valid'?); (5) no hierarchical decomposition — ALAS's blueprint is a flat DAG, while JORDAN needs recursive decomposition for deeply nested software tasks; (6) the learning feedback loop (execution to blueprint) is manual rather than automated; (7) ALAS's evaluation is on classical OR benchmarks — not software engineering benchmarks (SWE-bench, GAIA).
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Extend the Workflow Blueprinting layer to handle open-ended software development tasks — define workflow templates for common JORDAN task types (research, code, review, test, deploy); (2) Define validators for software artifacts: compilation check, test pass, lint compliance, schema validation, diff review — these are the JORDAN equivalents of ALAS's schedule constraints; (3) Implement compensation handlers for software tasks: code that fails tests triggers retry with different approach; research timeout triggers fallback to cached knowledge; review rejection triggers revision with reviewer feedback; (4) Add tool integration to the Agent Factory — JORDAN agents need tool access (search, execute, read/write) in addition to LLM reasoning; (5) Make the versioned log integrate with git — JORDAN's artifacts are code changes, and the log should track which commit was produced by which agent with what validation results; (6) Use ALAS's localized repair strategy directly in JORDAN's replan node — when a sub-task fails, repair only the affected dependency region rather than replanning the entire project; (7) Adopt the independent validator pattern — JORDAN's risk_assessment node should verify agent outputs in a fresh context to avoid self-verification failures.
validation_context: Validated on industrial-standard job-shop scheduling benchmarks (DMU, TA suites) and custom domains (urban ride sharing, event planning). Peer-reviewed across two arXiv papers (May and November 2025). Authors at Stanford University (Edward Y. Chang is a well-known researcher in AI and data systems, formerly at Google). The DMU/TA benchmarks are standard in operations research, providing credible comparison baselines (OR-Tools, CP-SAT). Code and seeds released. The November 2025 paper adds engine parity tests (ASL, Argo Workflows) and a conformance matrix. Not validated on software engineering or web agent benchmarks.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Compensation policy gaps — a disruption pattern not covered by any predefined handler causes escalation to human or blueprint replan, which may be slow; (2) Repair radius expansion — for highly coupled workflows, local repair may cascade to global replan, negating the local compensation benefit; (3) Validator false positives — if the validator incorrectly rejects valid agent outputs, unnecessary compensation is triggered, wasting compute; (4) Validator false negatives — if the validator misses an invalid output, errors propagate to downstream agents before detection; (5) Blueprint mismatch — if the workflow template does not match the problem structure, decomposition produces inefficient agent roles; (6) LLM nondeterminism — multiple runs of the same agent on the same input may produce different outputs, causing inconsistency in the versioned log; (7) Dependency on manual policy design — compensation handlers require domain expertise to define correctly, and poorly designed handlers may over-compensate or under-compensate; (8) The system has not been tested on adversarial scenarios where an attacker deliberately injects disruptions.
where_still_used: Primarily a research artifact from Stanford University as of late 2025. The code is released alongside the arXiv papers. [uncertain] No known production deployments. The architecture's compilation to enterprise workflow engines (Amazon States Language, Argo Workflows) suggests a path to production adoption, but no case studies exist yet. The approach has attracted attention in the multi-agent LLM planning community for its principled treatment of disruption recovery. Stanford's data systems group (Edward Chang) may pursue commercialization or broader deployment.
